description: Roast level modifiers. Defines how light, medium, and dark roasts behave differently and how to adjust brewing variables for each. Used by the decision tree to branch advice based on roast level.

light.visual: Light brown, no oil on surface, dry to touch

bean_properties.density: high — beans are hard and dense, less cellular breakdown during roasting
bean_properties.solubility: low — harder to extract, needs more energy (heat, time, surface area)
bean_properties.acidity: high — fruit acids preserved during shorter roast time
bean_properties.sweetness_potential: high — but only accessible with sufficient extraction

light.common_origins: Ethiopian, Kenyan, Colombian, light-roast Indian specialty (Attikan, Bibi plantation)

grind_size.why: Low solubility means you need more surface area to extract enough. Under-extraction is the default risk.

water_temperature.why: Dense beans need more thermal energy. Hoffmann advocates boiling water for light roasts. Heat loss during pour means slurry temp is always lower than kettle temp.

brew_time.why: Light roasts are forgiving on time — they resist over-extraction because there are fewer bitter compounds to extract.

ratio.why: No special adjustment needed. Ratio is about strength, not extraction.

agitation.why: More agitation helps extract stubborn light roast compounds. A good swirl or stir is beneficial.

common_problems.frequency: very common — the #1 light roast problem
common_problems.why: People use the same parameters as medium roast. Light roast needs more extraction energy.
Acidity extracts first but body compounds need more time/heat. Partial extraction gives brightness without weight.
Very underdeveloped roast (not the brewer's fault). Some light roasts are roasted too light — green, hay-like flavours indicate the roast itself was under-done.
fix_order: if still grassy, the roast is the problem — try a different roaster

light.expectations: A well-brewed light roast should taste: bright, fruity, complex acidity (like citrus or berries), floral or tea-like aromatics, with underlying sweetness. It should NOT taste sour, thin, or like lemon juice — that's under-extraction.

medium.visual: Medium brown, little to no oil on surface

bean_properties.density: moderate — some cellular breakdown, easier to extract than light
bean_properties.solubility: moderate — the goldilocks zone, works with standard parameters
bean_properties.acidity: moderate — some fruit acids remain, balanced by developing sweetness
bean_properties.sweetness_potential: highest — best balance of caramelization without carbonization
bean_properties.bitterness_potential: low-moderate — some Maillard products, pleasant chocolate/caramel notes

medium.common_origins: Most Indian specialty coffee (Chikmagalur, Coorg, Araku), Brazilian, Central American

grind_size.why: Medium roast is what most recipes are calibrated for. Use the recipe's default grind.

water_temperature.why: Moderate solubility means standard temps work well. No special adjustment needed.

brew_time.why: Hit the target brew time for your method and you'll be in the right range.

agitation.why: Medium roast is the baseline. Whatever the recipe says is correct.

common_problems.why: Medium roast sweetness fades fast with staleness. Check roast date first.
fix_order: check freshness (use within 2-4 weeks of roast)

common_problems.why: Medium-light sits close to light roast territory. May need a small extraction bump.
Medium-dark is approaching dark roast solubility. May be extracting a bit much.

medium.expectations: A well-brewed medium roast should taste: sweet (chocolate, caramel, brown sugar), balanced acidity (not sharp), full body, clean finish. This is the most approachable roast level for most people.

dark.visual: Dark brown to almost black, oily/shiny surface

bean_properties.density: low — significant cellular breakdown, beans are porous and fragile
bean_properties.solubility: very high — extracts easily and quickly, over-extraction is the default risk
bean_properties.acidity: very low — most fruit acids destroyed during extended roasting
bean_properties.sweetness_potential: low — many sugars have carbonized past the sweet point
bean_properties.bitterness_potential: high — lots of Maillard products, carbonized compounds, phenols

dark.common_origins: Traditional Indian coffee (estate blends, commercial roasts), Italian-style blends, French roast, most supermarket coffee

grind_size.why: High solubility means fines extract extremely fast. Coarser grind slows extraction and reduces harsh compound dissolution.

water_temperature.why: This is the MOST important adjustment for dark roasts. Hot water + high solubility = instant over-extraction. Lowering temp is the first move, not grind. This is the exception to the normal 'grind first' priority.

brew_time.why: Dark roasts extract fast. Extended contact time pulls out more harsh, ashy compounds.

ratio.direction: can go slightly stronger (1:14 to 1:16) for filter
ratio.why: Dark roasts produce a lighter-bodied cup per gram because so much mass was lost to CO2 during roasting. Slightly more coffee compensates.

agitation.why: High solubility + agitation = rapid over-extraction. Keep it gentle.

common_problems.frequency: very common — the #1 dark roast problem
common_problems.why: People use light/medium roast parameters (boiling water, fine grind, long steep). Dark roast is already highly soluble — standard params massively over-extract.
common_problems.important: For dark roasts, temperature comes BEFORE grind in the fix priority. This is the one exception to the normal adjustment order.

common_problems.why: Some level of smoke/char is inherent to dark roast — it's part of the flavour profile. But if it's overwhelming, it's either the roast itself (too dark) or over-extraction amplifying it.
fix_order: if still too smoky, the roast is darker than you prefer — try a medium-dark instead

common_problems.why: Extended roasting destroys volatile aromatics and fruit acids. Very dark roasts have a narrower flavour range — mostly chocolate, smoke, and roast character. This is a property of the roast, not a technique problem.
fix_order: try a medium or medium-dark roast if you want more complexity
there's no technique fix for flavour that was destroyed during roasting

dark.expectations: A well-brewed dark roast should taste: chocolatey, full-bodied, low acidity, smooth with some bitterness (pleasant, not harsh). Think dark chocolate, not burnt toast. Some smokiness is normal. If it tastes like an ashtray, it's over-extracted or the roast is too dark for your preference.

process_modifiers.description: Coffee processing method also affects how to brew. These are secondary modifiers on top of roast level.

washed.brewing_note: Standard parameters work well. Acidity is clear and structured.

natural.brewing_note: Can taste fermenty or boozy if over-extracted. Slightly coarser grind or lower temp can tame wild naturals. Sweetness comes through more easily than washed.

honey.flavour_tendency: sweet, syrupy body, moderate acidity, stone fruit
honey.brewing_note: Between washed and natural. Standard parameters usually work. Tends to be very forgiving.

monsooned.flavour_tendency: low acidity, heavy body, earthy, musty, spicy — uniquely Indian
monsooned.brewing_note: Treat like a dark roast even if the colour is medium. Monsooned beans are very porous and extract quickly. Use cooler water and coarser grind than the colour suggests.

freshness_guide.description: How roast level affects freshness window and resting period.

light.rest_after_roast: 7-14 days before peak flavour — light roasts need longer to degas
light.notes: Light roasts hold freshness longer because less cellular damage means slower CO2 loss. But they also need more resting time — brewing day-of-roast will taste papery and underdeveloped.

medium.notes: The standard freshness curve. Most recipes assume this timeline.

dark.rest_after_roast: 2-5 days — degasses fast due to porous structure
dark.notes: Dark roasts go stale fastest. The oils on the surface oxidize quickly. Use dark roasts sooner than you'd use light roasts. If they smell rancid or taste papery, they've gone off.

roast_switching_guide.description: What to change when switching between roast levels. Referenced by decision_tree.json → bean_switch node.

changes: Lower water temperature by 5-10C (from 96C+ down to 85-92C)
Reduce brew time if you control it (immersion methods)
light_to_dark.warning: This is the biggest roast jump. Using light roast parameters on dark roast will produce very bitter, harsh coffee. Reset everything.

changes: Increase water temperature by 5-10C (from 85-90C up to 96-100C)
Extend brew time if you control it
dark_to_light.warning: Using dark roast parameters on light roast will produce very sour, thin, underdeveloped coffee. Reset everything.
